topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s pronouncement echoed in the cavernous, dust-moted drawing-room, a sound that seemed to magnify the inadequacy of my position. I stood rigidly by the mantelpiece, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white islands in the gloom. My brother, Mark, sauntered in, radiating an easy confidence that felt like a physical slap. He surveyed the decaying grandeur of Grandmother’s estate with a knowing smirk. “Well, isn’t this a mess?” he drawled, his voice dripping with practiced amusement. I managed a tight nod, my gaze fixed on a chipped porcelain figurine, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole. Every glance he spared me felt like an accusation, a spotlight illuminating every perceived failing. I retreated to the library, the heavy velvet curtains doing little to soften the sharp edges of my unease.

[story 2]

The air in the old house hung thick and stale, smelling of lavender and disuse. I kept my shoulders hunched, pulling my cardigan tighter around myself as I watched Mark effortlessly charm the estate agent into overlooking the structural issues. When he spoke of plans for the property—plans that clearly positioned him as the inheritor and manager—a sour knot tightened in my stomach. I kept my voice deliberately flat when I offered a suggestion about the garden, a mumbled aside that was instantly swallowed by his booming laughter. He turned, his expression one of patronizing pity, and I could feel the heat rising in my neck. I focused intently on polishing a tarnished silver frame, my movements jerky and overly precise, desperate to disappear into the polished metal, anything but remain visible under his scrutiny.

[story 3]

It was the sheer weight of expectation that pressed down on me. When the lawyers finalized the division, the reality settled in: I was left with the crumbling manor, and Mark, the golden boy, was taking the reins. I avoided his eyes entirely, focusing instead on cataloging the mildew stains on the wallpaper in the hallway. When he asked if I had any ideas for renovation, his tone suggested I was merely an accessory, a decorative afterthought. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. “I… I haven’t really thought about it,” I managed, the words sounding thin and brittle. I could feel the blood draining from my face, a hot flush spreading across my cheeks, wishing the floorboards would crack open and let me vanish into the shadows.
